title: National Minimum Wage Regulations 2015
chapters: SI 2015/621 · Made 12 March 2015 · In Force 6 April 2015 · Replaced NMW Regulations 1999 (SI 1999/584) · Made under NMWA 1998 ss.1–4, 51–54 · Rates updated annually by National Minimum Wage (Amendment) Regulations
desc: The National Minimum Wage Regulations 2015 provide the detailed framework for calculating whether the national minimum wage has been paid. They define the four types of work (salaried hours work, time work, output work, and unmeasured work), the pay reference period, what counts as pay for NMW purposes (and what is excluded), what counts as working time (including travel time between assignments, mandatory training, and time on call), the accommodation offset, and the record-keeping requirements. The Regulations replaced the 1999 Regulations and simplified the calculation framework while retaining the same fundamental structure. They underpin the NMWA 1998 by providing the computational detail that determines whether an employer has met the minimum wage obligation. The Regulations are critical for FM sector employers with complex shift patterns, travel between sites, uniform requirements, and accommodation arrangements.

sections.title: Four types of work and hours calculation
sections.summary: The NMW Regulations define four types of work for the purpose of calculating whether the minimum wage has been paid: (1) Salaried hours work (regs.21–29): work done under a contract with an ascertained or ascertainable annual salary for an ascertained or ascertainable basic number of hours per year, paid in equal instalments. The worker is treated as working the basic hours in each pay reference period unless they work more or fewer. (2) Time work (regs.30–37): work paid by reference to the time worked (including piece work where a time rate applies). Hours include all time when the worker is at the workplace and required to be available for work, and travel time between assignments during the working day. (3) Output work (regs.38–44): work paid entirely by reference to the number of pieces made, processed, or tasks done. A 'fair piece rate' must be set so that a worker working at an average rate would earn at least 120% of the NMW per hour. (4) Unmeasured work (regs.45–49): work that does not fall within any other category. A 'daily average agreement' may set the hours treated as worked each day. The pay reference period (reg.6) is one month or, if the worker is paid at shorter intervals, the shorter interval. Within each pay reference period, the employer must ensure that total pay divided by total hours equals or exceeds the applicable NMW rate.
sections.text:
The four types of work are:

1. Salaried hours work (regs.21–29): The worker has an annual salary for a fixed or ascertainable basic number of hours per year, paid in equal instalments at equal intervals. The worker is treated as working their basic hours in each pay reference period, subject to adjustments for absences and excess hours.

2. Time work (regs.30–37): The worker is paid for time worked. Hours include:
• all time at the workplace when required to be available for work
• time spent travelling between assignments during the working day (Whittlestone v BJP [2014])
• time spent on mandatory training
• time spent on security checks, handover, or other activities required by the employer
• time 'on call' at the workplace (but NOT time on call away from the workplace where the worker can sleep — Royal Mencap v Tomlinson-Blake [2021])

3. Output work (regs.38–44): The worker is paid by the piece or by the task completed. The employer must set a 'fair piece rate' — a rate that enables a worker working at the average rate to earn at least 120% of the applicable NMW rate per hour.

4. Unmeasured work (regs.45–49): Work not falling within any other category. The employer and worker may agree a 'daily average agreement' setting the hours treated as worked each day. If there is no agreement, every hour of work counts.

versions.description: Original NMW Regulations 1999 (SI 1999/584) established the four-type framework. Replaced by NMW Regulations 2015.
NMW Regulations 2015 (SI 2015/621) replaced the 1999 Regulations. Simplified the salaried hours work definition and calculation framework. Retained the same four-type structure.

leadingCases.name: Royal Mencap Society v Tomlinson-Blake [2021] UKSC 6
leadingCases.heldText: The Supreme Court held that care workers on sleep-in shifts are only entitled to the NMW for time when they are awake and actually working, not for the entire duration of the sleep-in shift. Workers who sleep at the workplace and are available to respond if needed are not 'working' for NMW purposes during the time they are asleep, even if they are required to remain on the premises.
leadingCases.significance: Landmark Supreme Court authority on sleep-in shifts — workers only paid NMW for time awake and working, not the entire shift

leadingCases.name: Whittlestone v BJP Home Support Ltd [2014] ICR 275
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that time spent travelling between clients' homes during the working day constitutes working time for NMW purposes. The employer's practice of paying only for time at client homes resulted in NMW underpayment.
leadingCases.significance: Established that travel time between assignments counts for NMW calculation — directly relevant to FM sector mobile workers

refs: reg.10–17 NMW Regs 2015 (what counts as pay and deductions)
reg.59 NMW Regs 2015 (record-keeping — records must be kept for 6 years)
National Minimum Wage (Amendment) Regulations (annual rate uprating)

sections.title: Accommodation offset
sections.summary: Where an employer provides accommodation to a worker, the accommodation offset determines how this is taken into account in the NMW calculation. The employer may charge up to the accommodation offset rate per day (currently £11.10 from 1 April 2026, reviewed annually). If the employer charges more than the offset, only the offset amount counts as a benefit to the worker — the excess is treated as a deduction from pay, potentially bringing the effective hourly rate below the NMW. If the accommodation is provided free of charge, the offset amount is added to the worker's pay for calculation purposes. This provision is critical for employers who provide live-in accommodation, particularly in hospitality, agriculture, and care sectors.
sections.text:
(1) Where in any pay reference period—
(a) the employer provides the worker with living accommodation, and
(b) the amount which the employer charges the worker in respect of that living accommodation does not exceed the accommodation offset rate for the pay reference period,
the employer shall be treated as having made a payment to the worker of an amount equal to the accommodation offset rate for the pay reference period, less any amount which the employer charges.

(2) The accommodation offset rate for a pay reference period is a daily rate multiplied by the number of days in the pay reference period.

(3) Where in any pay reference period the amount which the employer charges the worker in respect of living accommodation exceeds the accommodation offset rate, the excess shall be treated as a deduction from the worker's pay.

(4) The daily accommodation offset rate is reviewed annually alongside NMW rates.

versions.description: Original NMW Regs 2015 accommodation offset. Replaced the equivalent provision in the 1999 Regulations.
Accommodation offset rate increased to £11.10 per day (from £10.66) for the 2026/27 NMW year.

leadingCases.name: Commissioners for HMRC v Ant Marketing Ltd [2019] UKUT 0113 (TCC)
leadingCases.heldText: The Upper Tribunal considered deductions from wages that reduced pay below the NMW. Accommodation charges exceeding the offset rate are treated as deductions that reduce the effective hourly rate. Employers providing accommodation must ensure the net effect of charges does not bring the worker's pay below the applicable NMW rate.
leadingCases.significance: Clarified the interaction between accommodation charges and NMW compliance.

pendingAmendments: NMW/NLW rates updated annually — April 2026 rates: NLW £12.70 (21+), £10.30 (18-20), £7.90 (under 18), £7.90 (apprentice)
ERA 2025 — Fair Work Agency to assume HMRC NMW enforcement role (not yet in force)
LPC annual report informs rate recommendations

parliamentaryOrigin.madeBy: Secretary of State for Business, Innovation and Skills
parliamentaryOrigin.note: The NMW Regulations 2015 replaced the NMW Regulations 1999 (SI 1999/584). They provide the computational framework for determining whether the national minimum wage has been paid. The four-type work classification (salaried hours, time work, output work, unmeasured work) is retained from the 1999 Regulations but simplified. Rates are updated annually by the National Minimum Wage (Amendment) Regulations. The NLW was introduced from April 2016 for workers aged 25+ (later reduced to 21+ from April 2024). The NMW/NLW enforcement framework (HMRC enforcement, penalties, naming and shaming) is established in the NMWA 1998 — the Regulations provide the calculation detail.

precedingDocuments.title: National Minimum Wage Act 1998
precedingDocuments.significance: The parent Act establishing the national minimum wage and providing the enabling powers under which the NMW Regulations are made. See nmwa1998.json for full analysis.

precedingDocuments.title: NMW Regulations 1999 (SI 1999/584)
precedingDocuments.publisher: Secretary of State for Trade and Industry
precedingDocuments.significance: The predecessor regulations replaced by the NMW Regulations 2015. Established the four-type work classification framework.

precedingDocuments.title: Low Pay Commission Annual Reports
precedingDocuments.significance: The LPC recommends annual NMW/NLW rate changes. Its reports inform the government's rate-setting decisions and are the primary evidence base for NMW policy.

leadingCases.name: Royal Mencap Society v Tomlinson-Blake [2021] UKSC 6
leadingCases.heldText: Sleep-in care workers are only entitled to NMW for time awake and actually working, not the entire sleep-in shift.
leadingCases.significance: Landmark Supreme Court authority on sleep-in shifts and NMW.

leadingCases.name: Whittlestone v BJP Home Support [2014] ICR 275
leadingCases.heldText: Travel time between clients' homes during the working day is working time for NMW purposes.
leadingCases.significance: Travel between assignments counts for NMW — critical for FM sector mobile workers.

leadingCases.name: Commissioners for HMRC v Ant Marketing [2019] UKUT 0113
leadingCases.heldText: Accommodation charges exceeding the offset rate are deductions that can reduce pay below NMW.
